evals.prompt: Review this Python code for quality issues: def calc(a, b): if a > 1000: return b * 0.1 else: return b * 0.2
evals.expected_output: Identifies magic numbers (1000, 0.1, 0.2), missing type hints, missing docstring, unclear function name, and suggests improvements with named constants.

evals.prompt: Refactor this JavaScript to follow DRY principle: function getUserName() { return fetch('/api/user').then(r => r.json()).then(d => d.name); } function getUserEmail() { return fetch('/api/user').then(r => r.json()).then(d => d.email); }
evals.expected_output: Extracts common fetch logic into reusable function, handles errors properly, uses async/await for clarity.

evals.prompt: Identify code smells in this class: class Order { process() { validate(); save(); notify(); log(); } validate() { ... } save() { ... } notify() { ... } log() { ... } }
evals.expected_output: Identifies potential violations of Single Responsibility Principle - class handles validation, persistence, notification, and logging. Suggests extracting into separate classes/services.

evals.prompt: Review this Rust code for error handling: fn read_file(path: &str) -> String { std::fs::read_to_string(path).unwrap() }
evals.expected_output: Identifies unsafe unwrap() usage, suggests proper error handling with Result type, recommends custom error type or anyhow/thiserror.

evals.prompt: Check this Go code for quality: func process(items []Item) { for i := 0; i < len(items); i++ { handle(items[i]) } }
evals.expected_output: Identifies range loop opportunity, suggests idiomatic Go patterns, checks for error handling in handle function.
assertions: Suggests range loop (for _, item := range items)
